mnemonics.title: Cloud Security Layers
mnemonics.content: NNHA: Network, No Data Should Be Compromised - Network, Host, Hypervisor, Application (the layers that need protection in cloud)

mnemonics.title: OWASP Top 10记忆
mnemonics.content: A S I D U A R E C O: A1-Injection, A2-Broken Auth, A3-Sensitive Data Exposure, A4-XML External Entities, A5-Broken Access Control, A6-Security Misconfiguration, A7-XSS, A8-Insecure Deserialization, A9-Using Components with Vulnerabilities, A10-Insufficient Logging

mnemonics.title: IAM Best Practices
mnemonics.content: PAPER: Principle of Least Privilege, Audit access, Policies are granular, Enforce MFA, Rotate credentials regularly

acronyms.usage: Isolated virtual network in cloud for secure resource deployment
Protects web apps from application-layer attacks like SQLi and XSS
Framework for managing user identities and access permissions
Requires multiple verification methods for added security

acronyms.expansion: Intrusion Detection System / Intrusion Prevention System
acronyms.usage: Monitors and blocks malicious network activity
Automatically detects and remediates cloud misconfigurations
Protocol for encrypting data in transit
Attack that overwhelms services with traffic

visualTips.visualization: Draw three-layer cake: Internet Gateway (top), Public Subnets (middle with load balancers), Private Subnets (bottom with app servers and databases). Show NAT Gateway connecting public to private.
Think of castle moat: Perimeter (firewall) → Network (VPC/subnets) → Host (OS hardening) → Application (WAF) → Data (encryption). Each layer provides additional protection.
Security Groups = bouncer at individual doors (instance-level, stateful). Network ACLs = ticket checker at building entrance (subnet-level, stateless). Both needed for defense in depth.
Draw a maze with check points at every corridor junction. Just because you entered the building doesn't mean you can access every room - verify at every step.

analogies.analogy: VPC is like a gated community - private roads (subnets), controlled entry points (security groups), and walls separating different areas (network ACLs) - all within the larger city (cloud).
Security groups are like bouncers at a club - they check who's allowed in/out, remember who's inside (stateful), and can be configured for specific rules.
Like giving office key cards - a janitor gets door access to cleaning closets, not the server room; executives get boardroom access, not necessarily IT infrastructure access.
Like bank security: Security guard (perimeter) → ID check (network) → Vault combination (host) → Safe deposit box (application) → Your lockbox (data). Multiple layers protect the same asset.

WAF is like a metal detector and bag search at airport - scanning all incoming 'people' (requests) for 'weapons' (malicious patterns like SQL injection) before allowing entry to the building (application).
